claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 9 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 9 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit. I'll need to ask you some questions to guide you through the process.

First, let's start with your child:

**How old is your child?**

The age category matters for eligibility:
- Under 16: Generally qualifies (with some conditions)
- 16-19: May qualify if in approved education or training
- 20 or over: Generally does not qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have more than one child you'd like to check, let me know about them too!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex is 9 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Alex is 9 years old, so they fall into the under-16 category. Let me proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Since Alex is 9 years old, let me ask the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

For a 9-year-old, this is typically straightforward - most children this age are not in paid employment. Can you confirm that Alex is either not working or working less than 24 hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex doesn't work at all. They're just a regular 9-year-old at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

In other words, can you confirm that Alex is not currently receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in their own name? 

For a typical 9-year-old at school, this would normally not apply, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name. They're just a child living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now let me check about apprenticeships:

**Is Alex NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is only 9 years old and in regular school, I expect they are not in any apprenticeship. Can you confirm that Alex is not in any type of apprenticeship program?

(For context: Certain apprenticeships in England, Scotland, and Wales can affect eligibility, but this typically only applies to older teenagers, not 9-year-olds.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any apprenticeship - they're only 9 and just going to primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status. This is an important eligibility requirement.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit?**

You qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** - this is the most common situation
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself and family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** Most UK residents will meet the first criterion - do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now let me check about your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You qualify if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** - the most common situation
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be towards their care - this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds Child Benefit payments

You mentioned Alex lives at home with you, so it sounds like you live with Alex. Can you confirm this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- A grandparent or other family member
- Anyone else who might be claiming

If someone else is claiming but you've both agreed that you should claim instead, that's also important to know.

What's the situation - is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's living situation to see if any special rules apply.

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current living situation?**

- Is Alex in hospital?
- Is Alex in local authority care?
- Is Alex fostered?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you?

Based on what you've told me (that Alex lives at home with you and is in primary school), it sounds like Alex is living normally at home. Can you confirm this is the case?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex is just living normally at home with me. They're not in hospital, care, or fostered or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, can you confirm that your local council is not currently paying for Alex's accommodation or upkeep? 

Since Alex lives at home with you and you're responsible for them, I expect the answer is that the council is not paying, but I need to confirm this to complete the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. I'm supporting them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Excellent News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements** for Child Benefit for Alex. Here's why:

✅ **Alex is a qualifying child** (9 years old, under 16)  
✅ **Alex is not working full-time** (not working at all)  
✅ **Alex is not receiving benefits** in their own right  
✅ **Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship**  
✅ **You meet UK residency requirements** (living in Manchester)  
✅ **You are responsible for Alex** (living together)  
✅ **No one else is claiming** Child Benefit for Alex  
✅ **Alex is living normally at home** with you  
✅ **The local council is not paying** towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance  

---

### **Next Steps to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay a tax charge
5. **Payments**: You'll receive payments every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: **https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim**

Do you have any questions about the application process or the High Income Child Benefit Charge?

reasonings: Alex is 9 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant who resides in Manchester (UK), the claimant is responsible for Alex by living with them, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all UK Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 9-year-old child Alex, including having a qualifying child, meeting UK residency requirements, being responsible for the child, and having no disqualifying circumstances.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 9 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant who resides in Manchester (UK), the claimant is responsible for Alex by living with them, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all UK Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 9-year-old child Alex, including having a qualifying child, meeting UK residency requirements, being responsible for the child, and having no disqualifying circumstances.
